title: Chapter 20: The View From Here
content: Volume Two: Finale
<!-- Speed: Breather | D12+D12 -->
They ended up on Chloe's roof.

Not a metaphor. The actual roof of Chloe's apartment building in the Mission — accessible via a fire escape that had been "temporarily" out of compliance since 2014. They'd climbed it with burritos and a six-pack of the cheap beer that Sam had once described as "tasting like poor decisions" but now described as "tasting like road trips."

The city spread below them: the Bay Bridge lit up like a necklace, the fog rolling in over Twin Peaks, the distant glow of Salesforce Tower reminding everyone that somewhere, someone was still checking email at 9 PM.

"I used to come up here after bad days," Chloe said. "After the 36-hour sprints. After Dave stole my slides. After I cried in the bathroom and then went back to my desk because I didn't know what else to do."
"What did you think about?"
"I thought about leaving. Every night, I thought about leaving. And every morning, I went back anyway."
Sam opened a beer. "What changed?"
"Nothing changed. That was the problem. Nothing was ever going to change unless I made it change, and making it change meant doing something that felt insane."
"Like calling your CEO a dark lord on a company-wide Zoom."

Sam didn't say anything. She was looking at her phone — not at the spreadsheet, but at a photo. Chloe leaned over. It was a picture from the strawberry farm: Chloe, foam sword raised, poncho billowing, charged at the pink windmill. The photo had been taken by a stranger who'd posted it to TikTok. 4 million views.
"I took a better one," Sam said.
She swiped. The next photo: Chloe, asleep in the passenger seat of Shadowfax, crown slightly askew, mouth slightly open — the kind of vulnerable a person only is around someone they trust completely.
"That's the one that matters," Sam said.
"That's the one no one else saw."
"That's why it matters."

The fog rolled in properly now — the aggressive kind that swallows entire neighborhoods. It erased the Bay Bridge first, then the skyscrapers. Soon it was just the two of them on a rooftop, the world reduced to the only things that mattered.

"Volume two," Chloe said to the fog, "began with a pretender queen and an algorithmic betrayal. It proceeded through a counter-reformation of liberated souls. It peaked with a keynote. It concluded with a return to the old country."
Sam didn't look up. "Talking to the readers again."
"Yes."
"What's the closing line?"
Chloe considered. Below them, the fog had erased the city. Above them, the stars were doing what stars do — indifferent, ancient, constant.

"The kingdom was never a place," Chloe said. "It was a way of looking. It was the decision to call a gas station an alchemist's shrine and mean it. It was the decision to call a pink windmill a soul engine and charge at it with a foam sword and mean it. It was the decision to call your best friend your Royal Treasurer and mean it."
"I do keep the budget," Sam said.
"You keep the budget. You keep the spreadsheet. You keep me from getting arrested, which is a not-insignificant portion of the Royal Treasurer's duties."
"It's the most important portion."
"It's the second most important."
Sam looked at her. "What's the first?"
"You stayed."
Sam didn't say anything for a long time. Then she updated Column X of the spreadsheet — "Things That Actually Mattered In Volume Two" — with a single word: *Everything.*

Chloe stood up and walked to the roof's edge. Not dangerously — just to where the fog was thickest, where the city was invisible, where the only sounds were distant car alarms and the foghorns on the Bay.
The crown was on her head. The crack in it caught the light of Sam's phone and threw a tiny rainbow into the gray.
"What's next?" Sam asked.

"I don't know," Chloe said. "That's the whole point. Three weeks ago, I knew exactly: 9 AM standup, 10 AM client call, 11 AM cry in the bathroom, 12 PM pretend everything was fine. Now I don't know. And not knowing is the best thing that's ever happened to me."
Sam closed the laptop. She stood beside Chloe at the roof's edge.
"Column Y," Sam said. "What's Next. Value: blank. Deliberately."
Chloe smiled. "That's my Royal Treasurer."
*End of Volume Two*
*...to be continued. Probably. The kingdom is portable and there are still a lot of windmills.*
